schools.summary: The beast from the sea: 'and I stood upon the sand of the sea, and saw a beast rise up out of the sea, having seven heads and ten horns, and upon his horns ten crowns, and upon his heads the name of blasphemy' — Barnes (with Gill) holds the beast is the Roman power, specifically the civil/secular Roman power (the fourth beast of Daniel 7) that supported the church of Rome (the historicist reading taking the beast as imperial/papal Rome, the persecuting world-power, the 'seven heads' being 'seven mountains,' 17:9, the seat of Rome); the 'ten horns' with 'ten crowns' marking the beast's royal power and the kingdoms within it (the same heads/horns as the dragon, 12:3, but now crowned on the horns); 'the name of blasphemy' on its heads marking its blasphemous claims and titles; the beast rising 'out of the sea' (the figure of the nations and peoples in commotion, 17:15), the persecuting world-power emerging from the turbulent mass of peoples (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Victorinus and Bede read the beast as the kingdom of Antichrist — the Roman Empire (the fourth beast of Daniel that afflicted the saints) as the seat and instrument of the final Antichrist; Victorinus shows the seven heads and ten horns/crowns are the same (the people mingled with the variety of the kingdom of that time of Antichrist), the beast the world-power through which the devil and antichrist make war on the saints, overcoming the body though not the soul.

debates.question: What is the beast that rises from the sea?

sides.position: Imperial/papal Rome — the secular Roman power supporting the church of Rome (historicist)
The Roman Empire / Nero and the persecuting state (preterist)
The kingdom of a future Antichrist (futurist; the fathers' reading)

themes: saw a beast rise up out of the sea, having seven heads and ten horns
upon his heads the name of blasphemy
the beast rising from the sea
the crux of the beast's identity (Rome or Antichrist)

schools.summary: The composite beast and its satanic source: 'and the beast which I saw was like unto a leopard, and his feet were as the feet of a bear, and his mouth as the mouth of a lion: and the dragon gave him his power, and his seat, and great authority' — the beast combining the features of the leopard, bear, and lion (Daniel 7:4-6, the first three beasts), gathering into itself the characteristics of all the preceding world-empires (the figure of the most terrible, comprehensive persecuting power, uniting the swiftness/cruelty of the leopard, the strength/ferocity of the bear, and the devouring power of the lion, in the historicist reading Rome inheriting and surpassing the powers before it); 'the dragon gave him his power, and his seat (throne), and great authority' — the beast deriving its power, throne, and authority from the dragon (Satan, 12:9), the persecuting world-power Satan's instrument, energized and authorized by him, the visible agent of the dragon's hostility to God and the church (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: the beast which I saw was like unto a leopard, and his feet were as the feet of a bear, and his mouth as the mouth of a lion
the dragon gave him his power, and his seat, and great authority
the composite beast empowered by the dragon
Satan's visible instrument combining Daniel's empires

schools.summary: The deadly wound healed: 'and I saw one of his heads as it were wounded to death; and his deadly wound (death-stroke) was healed: and all the world wondered after the beast' — one of the beast's heads mortally wounded yet the deadly wound healed (the figure of the persecuting power suffering an apparently fatal blow and then reviving); interpreters applying it variously — the fall and revival of the Roman power, the wounding of the empire and its revival in the papal form (Gill: the secular Roman power wounded by the empire's fall, reviving as papal Rome, 'introducing popish idolatry, the same in substance, only in a new form'), or the preterist Nero-redivivus expectation; 'all the world wondered after (followed in wonder) the beast' — the whole world, astonished at the beast's revival, following and admiring it, the apparent invincibility of the revived power drawing the world's wondering allegiance (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Gill).

themes: one of his heads as it were wounded to death; and his deadly wound was healed
all the world wondered after the beast
the beast's deadly wound healed and the world's wonder
the persecuting power revived and admired

schools.summary: The double worship: 'and they worshipped the dragon which gave power unto the beast: and they worshipped the beast, saying, Who is like unto the beast? who is able to make war with him?' — the world worshiping both the dragon (Satan, the source of the beast's power) and the beast itself (the homage paid to the persecuting power being, in effect, the worship of Satan behind it, idolatry directed to the beast and its author, the beast-worship satanic idolatry, the world's homage to the antichristian power the worship of its diabolical source); 'Who is like unto the beast? who is able to make war with him?' a blasphemous parody of the praise due to God ('Who is like unto thee, O LORD?' Exodus 15:11), the beast's seeming invincibility and irresistible power drawing an acclamation that belongs to God alone — the deification of brute power (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

themes: they worshipped the dragon which gave power unto the beast: and they worshipped the beast
Who is like unto the beast? who is able to make war with him?
the worship of the dragon and the beast
the deification of the persecuting power, a blasphemous parody of God's praise

schools.summary: The blasphemous mouth and the appointed reign: 'and there was given unto him a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies; and power was given unto him to continue forty and two months' — the beast given a mouth uttering great (arrogant, boastful) things and blasphemies (drawn from Daniel 7:8, 25, the little horn's 'great words against the Most High'), the beast's characteristic arrogance and blasphemy against God (the proud self-exaltation and blasphemous claims and titles, in the historicist reading the arrogant pretensions of the papal power assuming divine titles and authority); 'power... to continue forty and two months' the same period as elsewhere (11:2; 12:6, 14), read by historicists as 1260 years of the beast's dominion, by preterists/futurists as a literal 3.5 years; the beast's authority real but bounded by God's appointed limit, 'was given' stressing that even the beast acts only by divine permission (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies
power was given unto him to continue forty and two months
the beast's blasphemous mouth and 42-month reign
the arrogant persecuting power reigning for the appointed time by God's permission

schools.summary: The threefold blasphemy: 'and he opened his mouth in blasphemy against God, to blaspheme his name, and his tabernacle, and them that dwell in heaven' — the beast blaspheming God himself, his name (his being and character), his tabernacle (his dwelling, taken as heaven, or as the church, the dwelling of God by his Spirit), and those who dwell in heaven (the angels and the redeemed) — the comprehensive blasphemy of the antichristian power against all things divine and holy; 'his name' God's revealed character and majesty, 'his tabernacle' God's dwelling-place/sanctuary/people (the beast reviling both God and his sanctuary); 'them that dwell in heaven' the angels and glorified saints (the beast's blasphemy reaching even to the heavenly inhabitants, in the historicist reading the papal blasphemy including the perversion of the worship of saints and angels, or the reviling of the true heavenly church) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: he opened his mouth in blasphemy against God, to blaspheme his name, and his tabernacle, and them that dwell in heaven
the beast's blasphemy against God, his tabernacle, and heaven
the comprehensive blasphemy of the antichristian power
reviling God, his dwelling, and the heavenly host

schools.summary: The war on the saints and universal dominion: 'and it was given unto him to make war with the saints, and to overcome them: and power was given him over all kindreds, and tongues, and nations' — the beast permitted to war against the saints (God's people) and to overcome them (drawn from Daniel 7:21, the little horn making war with the saints and prevailing), the persecuting power triumphing for a time over the faithful (the beast's overcoming the outward, bodily triumph of persecution, the killing and subduing of the faithful, yet the saints overcoming in spirit and faith even when bodily overcome); 'power... over all kindreds, and tongues, and nations' the beast's authority extending over all peoples (the fourfold designation marking the universal scope of its dominion, the world-wide reach of the persecuting power), yet 'was given' — its power permitted and bounded by God (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: it was given unto him to make war with the saints, and to overcome them
power was given him over all kindreds, and tongues, and nations
the beast warring on and overcoming the saints, with universal power
the world-wide persecuting dominion bounded by God's permission

schools.summary: The universal worship and the kept elect: 'and all that dwell upon the earth shall worship him, whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world' — all the earth-dwellers (the unregenerate world) worshiping the beast (the persecuting power receiving the homage of all save the elect, the near-universal apostasy and idolatry of the antichristian age); only those 'whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb' worshiping the beast (the elect, written in the Lamb's book of life, kept from the idolatry, the book distinguishing the faithful from the apostate world); 'the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world' — Christ's sacrifice, though accomplished in time, in God's eternal purpose from the foundation of the world (or the names written in the book from the foundation of the world), the redemption and election grounded in God's eternal counsel, the security of those written in the book resting on the Lamb's foreordained sacrifice (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Cyril: the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world marks the eternal purpose of God in Christ's sacrifice; those written in the book of life, secured by God's foreordained redemption, are kept from worshiping the beast — the elect preserved through the antichristian apostasy by the eternal counsel grounded in the slain Lamb.

themes: all that dwell upon the earth shall worship him
whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world
the world worshipping the beast, except those in the Lamb's book of life
the great apostasy and the elect secured by God's eternal purpose

schools.summary: The solemn summons: 'if any man have an ear, let him hear' — the same formula used in the seven letters (2:7, etc.) and by Christ in the Gospels (Matthew 11:15), here solemnly arresting attention before the weighty word that follows (v.10), the call to heed what is said about the beast and the patience of the saints; the call set here (and not, as in the letters, after a promise) marking the special importance of the following declaration about captivity, the sword, and the patience of the saints (the reader bidden to give earnest attention); 'if any man have an ear, let him hear' the universal call to heed (everyone who can hear called to give attention, the message about the beast and the saints' endurance for all, the summons underlining the gravity of the warning and comfort that follow) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: If any man have an ear, let him hear
the call to hear
the solemn summons before the word on the saints' endurance
the reader bidden to give earnest heed

schools.summary: The law of retribution and the saints' endurance: 'he that leadeth into captivity shall go into captivity: he that killeth with the sword must be killed with the sword. Here is the patience and the faith of the saints' — the persecutors who lead the saints into captivity and slay them with the sword themselves suffering the same (the principle of divine retribution, cf. Matthew 26:52; Genesis 9:6, the beast and its agents repaid in kind, God's justice requiting the persecutors); this assurance of retribution comforting the persecuted saints (though the beast triumphs for a time, its violence recoiling on itself, the saints enduring knowing God will avenge, the certainty of judgment on the oppressor sustaining the oppressed); 'here is the patience (patient endurance) and the faith of the saints' — this truth of certain retribution and God's justice the ground and occasion of the saints' patient endurance and faith (in persecution the saints called not to retaliate but to endure faithfully, trusting God's justice) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: He that leadeth into captivity shall go into captivity: he that killeth with the sword must be killed with the sword
Here is the patience and the faith of the saints
the law of retribution and the patience of the saints
God's certain retribution grounding the saints' faithful endurance

schools.summary: Gill (with the historicist tradition): the second beast 'coming up out of the earth' with 'two horns like a lamb' but speaking 'as a dragon' is the papal/ecclesiastical power — the spiritual or religious arm of antichristian Rome, the see of Rome with the pope, his clergy, cardinals, archbishops, bishops, and priests; lamb-like in its profession of meekness, Christlikeness, and religion, yet dragon-like in its speech (its true satanic character betrayed by its voice, the false meekness concealing the dragon's lies, the hypocrisy of the religious deceiver gentle in show but speaking the dragon's words). Barnes (reading 'the earth' as the Latin world) takes it as the papal spiritual power, distinct from but supporting the first (secular) beast.

The deceptive religious power: 'and I beheld another beast coming up out of the earth; and he had two horns like a lamb, and he spake as a dragon' — the second beast resembling a lamb (with the appearance of gentleness, meekness, and innocence, claiming Christlike/lamb-like character, the outward show of religion and mildness masking its true nature) but speaking as a dragon (its speech betraying its true, satanic character, the false meekness concealing a dragon's voice). The futurist reads the second beast as the false prophet (16:13; 19:20) of the end-time, the religious deceiver who serves the first beast and directs worship to it (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Cyril and the fathers: the lamb-horned, dragon-voiced beast is the antichristian deceiver who comes in sheep's clothing — gentle and Christlike in appearance but satanic in speech; the false prophet/deceiver of the last time, who by a counterfeit of meekness and religion leads men astray to worship the beast, his dragon's voice unmasking the wolf beneath the lamb's likeness.

debates.question: What is the second beast (from the earth)?

sides.position: The papal/ecclesiastical power — the pope with his clergy, the religious arm of antichristian Rome (historicist)
The false prophet of the end-time — the religious deceiver who serves the first beast (futurist)

themes: another beast coming up out of the earth; and he had two horns like a lamb, and he spake as a dragon
the second beast from the earth
the crux of the second beast's identity
the papal/ecclesiastical power, or the false prophet of the end

schools.summary: The second beast as agent of the first: 'and he exerciseth all the power of the first beast before him, and causeth the earth and them which dwell therein to worship the first beast, whose deadly wound was healed' — the second beast wielding the full authority of the first 'before (in the presence/on behalf of) him' (acting as its agent and promoter, the religious power serving the secular, exercising its authority and advancing its cause); its chief work to make the world worship the first beast (the false prophet directing the world's homage to the persecuting power, the religious deceiver enforcing the idolatry of the beast); the worship directed to the first beast 'whose deadly wound was healed' (v.3, the beast that recovered from its mortal wound, the second beast promoting the worship of the revived persecuting power, in the historicist reading the ecclesiastical power upholding and enforcing the worship of the revived papal Roman power) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: he exerciseth all the power of the first beast before him
causeth the earth and them which dwell therein to worship the first beast, whose deadly wound was healed
the second beast enforcing worship of the first
the false prophet directing the world's idolatrous homage

schools.summary: The lying wonders: 'and he doeth great wonders, so that he maketh fire come down from heaven on the earth in the sight of men' — the second beast working great signs to deceive, even making fire come down from heaven before men (the false prophet's lying wonders, cf. 2 Thessalonians 2:9, 'after the working of Satan with all power and signs and lying wonders'; Matthew 24:24, that authenticate his deception); the 'great wonders' the deceptive miracles by which the false prophet imposes on the world (whether real satanic wonders or pretended/lying signs, serving to deceive and to validate the worship of the beast, the counterfeit of true prophetic power); 'maketh fire come down from heaven... in the sight of men' a parody of Elijah's calling down fire (1 Kings 18; cf. 2 Kings 1), the imitation of the genuine prophetic sign used to deceive (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

themes: he doeth great wonders, so that he maketh fire come down from heaven on the earth in the sight of men
the second beast's lying wonders, even fire from heaven
the false prophet deceiving by counterfeit miracles
a parody of Elijah's sign to validate beast-worship

schools.summary: The deception and the commanded image: 'and deceiveth them that dwell on the earth by the means of those miracles which he had power to do in the sight of the beast; saying to them that dwell on the earth, that they should make an image to the beast, which had the wound by a sword, and did live' — the false prophet deceiving the world by the miracles it is permitted to work in the presence of (on behalf of) the beast (the lying signs, v.13, accomplishing their purpose, the world led astray); commanding the world to 'make an image to the beast' (a likeness, representation, or institution honoring the beast that was wounded and revived, the setting up of beast-worship in a visible image, recalling Nebuchadnezzar's golden image, Daniel 3, in the historicist reading an institution or system imposed to honor the antichristian power); the image made to the beast 'which had the wound by a sword, and did live' (again the revived beast, v.3, 12, the image honoring the persecuting power that survived its mortal wound, the deception centering on the worship of the revived beast through its image) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: deceiveth them that dwell on the earth by the means of those miracles
they should make an image to the beast, which had the wound by a sword, and did live
the false prophet deceiving the world and commanding an image of the beast
the visible institution of the worship of the revived beast

schools.summary: The animated image and the death-decree: 'and he had power to give life unto the image of the beast, that the image of the beast should both speak, and cause that as many as would not worship the image of the beast should be killed' — the false prophet animating the image, giving it the appearance of life (breath) and the power of speech (the deception heightened, the image seeming alive and speaking, whether by satanic art or imposture, made to seem a living, speaking thing, the world awed by it, in the historicist reading the enlivening of the institution/system of beast-worship made to speak with authority); the image made to 'cause that as many as would not worship the image of the beast should be killed' — a decree of death against all who refuse to worship the image (the enforced worship backed by lethal compulsion, recalling Daniel 3, the fiery furnace for those who would not worship Nebuchadnezzar's image, the persecution of the faithful who will not bow to the beast) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: he had power to give life unto the image of the beast, that the image of the beast should both speak
cause that as many as would not worship the image of the beast should be killed
the image given life and the death-decree against non-worshippers
enforced idolatry backed by lethal persecution

schools.summary: The mark imposed on all: 'and he causeth all, both small and great, rich and poor, free and bond, to receive a mark in their right hand, or in their foreheads' — the false prophet imposing the mark on all classes without exception (the comprehensive pairs, small/great, rich/poor, free/bond, marking the universal reach of the requirement, none exempt); the mark (charagma) received 'in their right hand, or in their foreheads' a visible token of allegiance to the beast, placed on the most conspicuous and active parts (a parody of the sealing of God's servants, 7:3; 14:1, and of the binding of God's word on hand and forehead, Deuteronomy 6:8, the mark of subjection to the beast, professing and practicing allegiance to it); the mark signifying open allegiance to and identification with the beast (whether a literal mark, or in the historicist/symbolic reading the profession and badge of submission to the antichristian power, those who bear it belonging to the beast, in contrast to the sealed servants of God) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: he causeth all, both small and great, rich and poor, free and bond, to receive a mark in their right hand, or in their foreheads
the mark of the beast imposed on all
the badge of allegiance to the persecuting power
a parody of the sealing of God's servants

schools.summary: The economic coercion: 'and that no man might buy or sell, save he that had the mark, or the name of the beast, or the number of his name' — none allowed to engage in commerce (buy or sell) unless bearing the mark, the name of the beast, or the number of its name (the false prophet enforcing allegiance to the beast by economic coercion, exclusion from trade and livelihood for all who refuse the mark); the 'mark,' the 'name of the beast,' and 'the number of his name' three equivalent forms of the same badge of allegiance (to bear any being to profess subjection to the beast, the number of his name, v.18, a particular form of this identification); by barring the unmarked from buying and selling, the beast-power compelling submission through want and ruin (the faithful who refuse excluded from the ordinary means of life, the persecution operating not only by death, v.15, but by economic strangulation, in the historicist reading the exclusion of those who would not conform to the antichristian system) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: that no man might buy or sell, save he that had the mark, or the name of the beast, or the number of his name
the economic coercion enforcing the mark
allegiance to the beast enforced by economic strangulation
the faithful excluded from trade and livelihood

schools.summary: The number of the beast: 'Here is wisdom. Let him that hath understanding count the number of the beast: for it is the number of a man; and his number is Six hundred threescore and six' — the riddle inviting the wise to reckon the number (a cryptogram to be solved by computation, gematria, by which letters have numerical values, the believer invited to discern the beast's identity through its number); Barnes favours 'Lateinos' (Lateinos — the Latin/Roman kingdom or man, Λ=30, α=1, τ=300, ε=5, ι=10, ν=50, ο=70, ς=200 = 666), an ancient suggestion of Irenaeus, the number marking the Latin/Roman power and fitting the historicist identification of the beast with Rome; 'the number of a man' meaning either a number reckoned as men reckon (the ordinary human method of gematria) or the number belonging to a particular man/the man embodying the beast; 666, falling short of the perfect seven thrice over, marking the beast as supremely imperfect, the climax of fallen, human, antichristian power (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

The preterist computation: 'Nero Caesar' — whose Hebrew letters (Neron Kesar, נרון קסר) sum to 666, and 'Nero Kesar' (without the final n) to 616, explaining the ancient textual variant 616 found in some manuscripts; the number naming the emperor Nero, the persecuting head of the Roman beast. Other computations have been offered (various names summing to 666), but the chief contenders remain Lateinos (the Latin/Roman power, historicist) and Nero Caesar (preterist), the 616 variant weighing for the Nero reading.

debates.question: How is the number of the beast, 666, to be computed?

sides.position: 'Lateinos' — the Latin/Roman kingdom (the Greek letters sum to 666); Irenaeus's ancient suggestion (historicist)
'Nero Caesar' — the Hebrew gematria summing to 666 (and to 616 without the final n, explaining the textual variant); the emperor Nero (preterist)

themes: Let him that hath understanding count the number of the beast
it is the number of a man; and his number is Six hundred threescore and six
the number of the beast, 666
the crux of its computation (Lateinos vs Nero Caesar)
